articles.title: Two Brawls in Nassau: What Actually Happens When Cruise Passengers Get Arrested in the Bahamas
articles.excerpt: Six American passengers fined more than $52,000 USD after a June 8, 2026 brawl at Nassau Cruise Port escalated to a chair through a glass door at the Central Police Station; a second 12-passenger brawl at Royal Caribbean's Royal Beach Club Paradise Island followed within the week. Decoded: the Bahamian magistrate's-court process, what the cruise contract does (essentially nothing), what the US embassy can and cannot do, why travel insurance excludes criminal incidents, what happens to the rest of the family on the ship, and the long-term consequences of a foreign conviction. Pastoral framing on what to do when somebody in your party crosses a line abroad.
keywords: Royal Beach Club Paradise Island incident

articles.title: The First-Name Watch: Tropical Storm Arthur and What 10 Percent Odds Actually Mean for Cruisers
articles.excerpt: The National Hurricane Center is watching a tropical disturbance in the northwestern Gulf with a 10 percent chance of becoming Tropical Storm Arthur — the first named storm of the 2026 Atlantic season. Florida and the Caribbean are not threatened. This is the first real test of the hurricane-season checklist this site published before season open: what cruise lines actually do at 10 percent formation odds (essentially nothing visible), how to read NHC outlooks without panic, how the watch fits the broader below-average season outlook, and the first-name pattern across recent seasons. Pastoral framing: calibrated attention, not alarm.

articles.title: The Scooter in the Casino: What the Jewel of the Seas Lawsuit Actually Says About Cruise Accessibility
articles.excerpt: Colleen Parsons, a Florida resident, filed a negligence lawsuit against Royal Caribbean Group in Miami federal court on May 18, 2026 stemming from a June 22, 2025 fall over a parked mobility scooter in Casino Royale aboard Jewel of the Seas, fracturing her left wrist. Royal Caribbean's defense argues open and obvious hazard. Both parties are typically right in these cases. The real issue is upstream — accessibility-design choices the cruise industry has under-addressed. Decodes the legal framework (Miami forum, one-year statute, maritime reasonable care), the design problem, and the pastoral question of bearing one another's burdens at sea.

articles.title: When the Lights Go Out: The Caribbean Princess Power Loss, June 5, 2026
articles.excerpt: The Caribbean Princess — a 20-year-old, 112,894 GT Grand-class Princess Cruises ship carrying up to 3,140 guests — lost main power and air conditioning twice on the morning of June 5, 2026 during her Eastern Caribbean return from San Juan to Port Canaveral. Combined outage about 1 hour 15 minutes. Emergency power systems held. Guests reported calm. The ship arrived at Port Canaveral on schedule June 7. The mechanical-disruption case study that completes the five-category cruise itinerary-risk framework on this site (alongside weather, regulatory, infrastructure, and SOLAS/rescue). Decodes the redundancy architecture, why AC fails first, what stays up on emergency power, and the calibrated reality of operating older ships in the modern fleet.

articles.title: Liberty of the Seas Returns from Royal Amplification: What the Refit Actually Changes
articles.excerpt: Liberty of the Seas — Freedom-class, 156,271 GT, 2007-built — returned to service in late May 2026 after a Royal Amplification dry-dock that ran April through late May. Third of three 2026 Amplifications alongside Ovation and Harmony. Six headline additions: free-standing Starbucks on the Royal Promenade; Izumi Teppanyaki on deck 4 (replacing the fleet's last Sabor); 24 new inside cabins where the Conference Center used to be; redesigned Lime & Coconut bar; pool deck refresh with casitas; age-12-plus escape room. Now sailing 7-night Norwegian fjords, Bruges, and Copenhagen from Southampton through summer 2026. Decodes what the refit actually changes, where Liberty lands relative to Freedom-class peers and Oasis-class fleet-mates, and the pattern across the three 2026 Amplifications that signals Royal Caribbean's intent to keep mid-life ships in product parity.
keywords: Liberty of the Seas Royal Amplification

articles.title: When the Atlantic Lights Up: Watching a Rocket Launch From a Cruise Ship
articles.excerpt: Florida's Space Coast launched 109 SpaceX missions in 2025 and is on track for 140+ in 2026 — a launch roughly every two to three days. Most fly east over the Atlantic, the same direction Caribbean and Bahamas cruises sail. The best seat in the world for a rocket launch is probably the cruise you already booked. What it actually looks like from a top deck at sea: the bright dot that grows a column, the slow rise that suddenly becomes fast, the silence that holds for ninety seconds before the rumble arrives, the twilight phenomenon when the plume glows pink and electric blue.

articles.title: Fruit Fly on the Bread: What CDC Actually Found on the Norwegian Dawn (And How to Spot It Yourself)
articles.excerpt: On March 29, 2026 the Norwegian Dawn failed a CDC Vessel Sanitation Program inspection in Tampa with a score of 84/100 (passing is 85). It was the first failed inspection of 2026 across 79 inspections. The public report names four findings — two fruit flies at the buffet (one resting on bread); pooled water with mold-like growth in the soda cabinet; cheese stored at 44-45°F when the CDC limit is 41°F; water dripping from an unknown source in a food prep area. This article reads the report as a cruiser self-defense playbook — what each violation tells you to watch for in your own buffet, on any ship, on any line.

articles.title: When the Cruise Ship Is the Coast Guard: Silver Whisper's Seven-Hour Pacific Rescue
articles.excerpt: On May 26, 2026 Silversea's Silver Whisper — 28,258 GT, 392 passengers, 295 crew, en route Papeete to Vancouver — diverted to rescue a solo sailor on the dismasted yacht April Alice, 420 nautical miles off Oregon. Seven hours of rough-sea transit; the first line passed across parted; the second held; the injured sailor aboard within an hour of arrival. Decoding SOLAS Chapter V Regulation 33 (the legal framework that obligated the diversion), the operational reality of cruise-ship rescue capability, and what passengers on a diverted sailing actually owe each other when a distress call comes through.

articles.title: Hurricane Season Opens June 1: The 72-Hour Cruiser Checklist
articles.excerpt: The Atlantic hurricane season opens June 1, 2026 and runs through November 30. NOAA released its outlook May 21 (8-14 named storms, 3-6 hurricanes, 1-3 major). AccuWeather's competing forecast is slightly higher (11-16 / 4-7 / 2-4). CSU's April outlook anchored the bottom at 13/6/2. El Niño emergence now at 82% near-term per CPC, 96-98% for December-February. Six action items for the 72-hour window for cruisers with Caribbean, Bahamas, or US East Coast sailings booked between June and November — what to do in the next three days that you can't easily do after June 1.

articles.title: Seward Terminal Delay: What the Hubbard Glacier Cancellation Reveals About Alaska 2026 Cruise Risk
articles.excerpt: A new $137 million Seward cruise terminal failed pre-opening inspection days before its May 14 debut. Four lines rerouted ships to Whittier on 24 hours notice. One Ovation of the Seas sailing lost Hubbard Glacier on the second-to-last day; passengers chanted 'We Want Hubbard' at Guest Services. Decoding what port-infrastructure failure means as a third category of cruise itinerary risk distinct from weather and mechanical — and what affected passengers actually got from each line.
keywords: Ovation of the Seas Hubbard Glacier

articles.title: Bahamas Election Day Alcohol Ban: Five Cruise Lines, Three Patterns
articles.excerpt: On May 12, 2026 the Bahamas suspended every alcohol license in the country between 8 AM and 6 PM under Section 99 of the Parliamentary Elections Act. Five major cruise lines, three response patterns: NCL delayed by a day, Carnival swapped to Grand Turk, Royal Caribbean and Disney and MSC kept their calls and went dry onshore. The strategic decode and what each decision tells you about how the next regulatory disruption will land.

articles.title: Cruise Itinerary Changes: What to Do When a Port Is Missed, Diverted, or Cancelled
articles.excerpt: Roughly one in eight Caribbean sailings and one in five Alaska sailings see at least one itinerary change. The cruise contract gives the line broad latitude. Travel insurance language is specific. A practical guide to the four scenarios — missed port, diverted sailing, full cancellation, embark-port substitution — what each is worth, and how to claim cleanly.

articles.title: Cruise Travel Insurance in 2026: What You Actually Need, How the Major Plans Compare
articles.excerpt: An ad-free comprehensive reference. Side-by-side coverage comparison of cruise line in-house plans (Royal Caribbean, Carnival, Norwegian, MSC, Princess, Holland America, Celebrity, Disney) and the major third-party insurers (Allianz, BHTP WaveCare, Travel Guard, Travelex, Nationwide, Generali, Seven Corners, IMG). Plus Medjet, credit card coverage, CFAR, and pre-existing condition waivers.

articles.title: Cruise Travel Safety: The Shore-Side Safety Net Every Traveler Should Set Up Before Sailing
articles.excerpt: The single most important piece of pre-cruise preparation isn't a packing list — it's a person back home with the right information. One trusted person back home with the right info can resolve almost any at-sea or in-port emergency through three institutional channels — the cruise line ship-to-shore desk, your country's consular service, and the embassy in the affected port. Ten minutes today. Could matter on the one day it does.

articles.title: Perfect Day Mexico, Rejected: What Royal Caribbean's $600M Setback Actually Means
articles.excerpt: Mexico's environment ministry blocked Royal Caribbean's $600M Perfect Day Mexico project on May 19, 2026, after a 4.8-million-signature petition and a direct statement from President Sheinbaum. Royal Caribbean's response says Round 2, not surrender. A $600 million investment commitment doesn't get walked away from after one regulatory setback. What this means for cruisers, the Mesoamerican Reef, and the future of private cruise destinations.

articles.title: RCL Diamond to MSC Voyagers Club: How the Status Match Actually Works in 2026
articles.excerpt: A practical guide to taking the MSC status match from Royal Caribbean Diamond — what the match actually delivers, what it doesn't, what to evaluate on your first MSC sailing, and what to do if the first sailing goes sideways. Written by an RCL Diamond cruiser who just completed his first MSC sailing on the match.

articles.title: MSC Seaside Service Review: The Apology That Wasn't a Plan
articles.excerpt: A cabin-steward case study from a 2026 MSC Seaside sailing. What service recovery looks like when 'it won't happen again' isn't paired with a structural fix. The status-match conversion-funnel critique MSC's loyalty team should read.

articles.title: Royal Caribbean vs MSC: Side by Side from the Deck
articles.excerpt: A category-by-category comparison written aboard MSC Seaside, a few weeks after a Radiance of the Seas sailing. Charitable to both lines; honest about where each shines and where each falls short. Verdict tilts Royal Caribbean overall on day-to-day service, communication, gangway accessibility, and tipping flexibility. MSC has genuine wins in embarkation, buffet, cabin space, and the international passenger mix.

articles.title: Cruise Ship Size, Explained: When Bigger Feels Smaller (and Vice Versa)
articles.excerpt: Gross tonnage is the headline number on every comparison chart. It's also the wrong number for predicting how a ship will feel onboard. The metric that matters is space ratio — and once you understand it, the chart reads very differently. Icon of the Seas at 248,663 GT and Radiance of the Seas at 90,090 GT have nearly identical space ratios.

articles.title: A Port Day That Doesn't Go Sideways: Using the Planner
articles.excerpt: Three failure modes wreck most port days — a too-tight reboarding buffer, a forgotten essential, and a budget that creeps. One printable planner handles all three. Two worked examples: a $169 Cozumel paid-excursion day, a $63 Nassau walking day.

articles.title: Five Nights on Radiance of the Seas: A Bahamas Logbook
articles.excerpt: A 5-night Bahamas trip aboard Radiance of the Seas from Port Everglades — Nassau, CocoCay, sea days. The crew who mattered (Jay in the MDR, Rother in Chops, Jelly at Izumi), Samba Grill as the standout meal, the ship at twenty-four, and an accessibility note grounded in sailing with a family member post-knee-replacement.

articles.title: Ten Days on Quantum of the Seas: A Pacific Coastal Logbook
articles.excerpt: A back-to-back logbook from Los Angeles. Two days at Cabo (whales, El Arco, Lover's Beach), one at Ensenada (North Star at port), the turnaround, and a second pass that ended with a male lion watching me through a diamond grate at Paipai Zoo Safari Park. Day-by-day notes, written honestly.

articles.title: What a Cruise Actually Costs in 2026: Building a Real Budget
articles.excerpt: The advertised fare is typically 40-45% of what a cruise actually costs. Three worked examples through the Budget Calculator — a 3-night Bahamas, a 7-night Western Caribbean, and a 12-night Mediterranean — show the real per-person-per-night math (between $417 and $531 across all three).

articles.title: Hurricane Season 2026 for Cruisers: What El Niño Actually Changes
articles.excerpt: Colorado State University's April forecast calls for a below-average Atlantic season (13 named, 6 hurricanes, 2 major). NOAA's official outlook releases May 21. What it actually means for Caribbean and Bahamas cruisers, what the cruise lines do when weather forces changes, and how to plan around it.

articles.title: Stateroom Sanity Check: The Cabin Mistakes Nobody Warns You About
articles.excerpt: Most cruise cabins are fine. The handful that aren't get assigned to people who didn't know what to look for — under pool decks, above theaters, near galleys, beside elevator banks. The Stateroom Sanity Check tool uses ship-specific exception data from real cruiser reports to catch them before you sail.

articles.title: Is the Drink Package Worth It? Using the Calculator to Find Your Real Break-Even
articles.excerpt: The '5 to 7 drinks per day' rule everyone repeats is wrong for the Royal Caribbean Deluxe package in 2026. Three worked examples through the In the Wake calculator — light, moderate, heavy — showing where a la carte still wins and where Deluxe actually pays off.

articles.title: Hantavirus on Cruise Ships 2026: What the MV Hondius Outbreak Actually Means
articles.excerpt: The MV Hondius Andes hantavirus outbreak in plain numbers: 9 confirmed cases, 3 deaths, one expedition ship out of Ushuaia. What it means for mainstream cruises (nothing) and expedition cruises (real, bounded). WHO, ECDC, CDC sourced.

articles.title: The Royal Beach Club Collection in 2026: What's Open, What's Coming, What It Changes
articles.excerpt: Paradise Island opened in December. Santorini opened in April. Cozumel opens at the end of the year. Royal Caribbean Group is going from two land-based destinations to eight by 2028 — what that means for itinerary planning.

articles.title: Mesoamerican Reef Diving from Costa Maya: A Cruiser's Guide
articles.excerpt: The Mesoamerican Barrier Reef sits less than half a mile offshore at Mahahual, the small fishing town a 10-minute taxi from the Costa Maya cruise pier. Local operators rotate through a working list of named coral-garden, drift, and wall sites paired into a comfortable two-tank morning dive. Banco Chinchorro — Mexico's largest platform-type reef complex and a UNESCO biosphere reserve — is a longer offshore trip from the same port.

articles.title: South Pacific Cruising in 2026: A First-Timer's Primer with Mystery Island at the Center
articles.excerpt: Australia's South Pacific market more than doubled into 2026. Mystery Island, Port Vila, Noumea, and Lifou anchor the standard 7-to-12 night loop from Sydney and Brisbane. Who sails it and how to plan a first sailing.

articles.title: Cruise Tipping for 2026: What You'll Actually Owe, by Line
articles.excerpt: 2026 cruise tipping by line: $17-$25 per guest per day, plus 18%-20% on bar, spa, specialty, and room service, plus optional cash for porters, butlers, and kids' club. All 15 major lines.

articles.title: Caribbean Cruise Trends for 2026: A First-Timer's Guide
articles.excerpt: What's actually changing in 2026 Caribbean cruising — capacity, ports, ships, shorter trips — verified against CLIA, AAA, and port-authority sources.

articles.title: Cruise Cabin Organization: Making Small Spaces Work
articles.excerpt: Magnetic hooks, over-door organizers, packing cubes, and the small comforts that turn a tight cabin into something livable.

articles.title: Cruise Tech & Photography Guide
articles.excerpt: Honest notes on tech and photography for cruises: waterproof pouches, e-readers, portable power, earbuds, and what actually helps you take better photos at sea.

articles.title: The Cruise Duck Tradition: How to Hide & Find Ducks
articles.excerpt: The hidden-duck tradition cruisers love — how to participate, what ducks to bring, and where to hide them.

articles.title: Solo in the Wake: The Practical Guide to Cruising Alone
articles.excerpt: Ship sizes, dining strategies, safety, solo supplements, and practical wisdom for every kind of solo traveler.

articles.title: Freedom of Your Own Wake
articles.excerpt: On choosing solitude without being alone.

articles.title: Why I Started Solo Cruising
articles.excerpt: From that first solo gangway to a thriving community — freedom found at sea.

articles.title: Top 20 First-Cruise Questions (Answered)
articles.excerpt: A first-person guide to planning, packing, ports, and ship life.

articles.title: Visiting (or Moving to) the United States
articles.excerpt: Warm, honest guidance to enjoy America deeply — scale, culture, dining, money, weather, and the art of one great day.

articles.title: In the Wake of Grief: When Loss Needs Water
articles.excerpt: Finding solace and healing in the steady rhythm of the sea.

articles.title: Accessible Cruising: Five Universal Principles for Disabled Travelers
articles.excerpt: Practical guidance for making cruise travel accessible and enjoyable for disabled travelers.
